item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Could not be sourced, because I did it myself with MSPaint…

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

EDIT: The image has been sourced and is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So there's a good chance one of the sold paintings make you see your significant other in the worst possible light. And someone bought it. The reason could think of: Someone who hates their spouse sees a horrendous painting that looks like an awful version of their hated spouse, and buys it just to be cruel to their spouse. Not realising that the spouse will see a horrible version of the buyer.

Is that really Keith Haring's style, though? I googled him, and his art doe snt really look like these paintings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tone issues coupled with the fact it is a Thing What Makes You Go Crazy make me give it a No Vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason I really like that the effect is completely nullified by ordinary eyeglasses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
so, what would someone want with a painting of their significant other in the "worse possible light" and a painting of the incarnation of what you would hate in a dating partner? are they in a bad relationship and enjoy how the paintings show that it could be so much worse than it is?

this article was very interesting. I want all 6 paintings for a personal art museum. the idea of paintings showing you perfection or horrible flaws depending on what you see is interesting, and I like the execution. over all, I don't see a reason I shouldn't upvote. so, since I can't justify not upvoting, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been thinking about this a bit recently, (I've been considering adding/modifying a bit) and I'm wondering if they would actually have the opposite effect. If you are having problems with your spouse, and then you go look at #3, you see them as perfect. As they could be. Then you go home and realize they are very imperfect humans. This could make the situation worse, as you realize how far short they fall.

Seeing yourself as perfect could do the same thing. #4 might actually make you feel better. You see yourself as horrible, then realize "Hey, I'm not really all that bad! I could be a lot worse."

Of course, this would take time to reflect on what you saw, and some people might not be able to get over what they saw, one way or the other.

What do you think? Expand, tweak or leave alone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the tone and execution could be a little better, but I think a lot of the ideas in here are pretty cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I must say, these four paintings are 3/4 awesome. #2 and #3 sound nearly harmless, though, which bugs me slightly. Hell, #2 would probably eliminate any need for marital or relationship counseling, and #3 sounds great for getting rid of depression. Whenever you start to hate your [l/w]ife, just look at SCP-1236-[2/3], problem solved. And I could certainly see weaponizing #4. Drop that puppy into a CI or CotBG hideout with a few handguns or cyanide pills, and boom, problem solved, send in the cleaners.

Definitely upvoted, really neat idea, and far as I see it, the most practically useful SCP I've read in a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone affected by SCP-547.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last paragraph makes it for me; I just can't stop wondering what the other two do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*takes a shot* I need to erase that guide and start from scratch.

This is one of the most straightforwardly memetic things in the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems kind of like something out of the Holder's series. Not sure if that's good or bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

RAGE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose it IS rather Bland

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Experimentation continues with sexual preferences outside of the perceived norm."

What a boring suggestion :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Experimentation continues with sexual preferences outside of the perceived norm."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This canvas will show a full-length portrait of a person who is the viewer’s ideal of physical perfection, based upon sexual orientation.

What if the viewer is bisexual/pansexual? Consider in particular that some bisexuals in particular don't care for androgyny.

This canvas features a portrait of the viewer’s current or most recent significant other.

What if they have more than one significant other? For instance, if they're polyamorous, or monogamous but cheating. (Yes, it's perfectly possible to not have a 'favorite' significant other.)

The more-than-one-SO thing I can buy not coming up in tests, but the 'one single ideal of perfection' thing strikes me as contrived - I can imagine a person having a theoretical ideal of perfection for themselves, but there's gotta be a lot of people who don't have a single ideal for others.

That aside… I kinda sorta like this idea, but I'm not sure what I think of the execution overall. No vote for now, but I will probably end up voting one way or another later, so I'll be interested to see if you change or adjust anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't actually given a huge amount of thought to the two missing paintings, but I like this idea. I had considered some ideas about locations, but perhaps what you said was what I subconsciously thinking. And it does make you wonder about the buyer, that's for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be added to the big list of overdone cliches: A Thing What Does Stuff (we have more than 1000 of these; try something less "SCP-y")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I had to guess, based on the fact that #4 is the opposite of #2, I'd say one of them shows your significant other at their worst, while the other shows the human embodiment of all that scares/disgusts you. Which makes me wonder about the buyer…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, what if the viewer was asexual? That'd be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking MC&D, too. It may be best to leave it undefined, and maybe make a connection in a tale instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DAMMIT REJECT I was going to say something like that.

This does feel very Are We Cool Yet?, but I can't see what the addition of a link could do to improve the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an object that has an anomalous effect! Can't say I've ever seen that before, no siree!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. I wonder… Are They Cool Yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not actually memetic, if you read "Understanding Memetics" in the "Guides" tab…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't that a little bit like saying, "Oh, look a humanoid SCP. Can't say I've ever seen that before, no siree!"

The ability to give something a preexisting tag doesn't make it less good…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A memetic picture SCP. Can't say I've ever seen that before, no siree!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…. Damn, good point on the VA.

As far as the significant other bit, I believe that's what the "most recent" part implies… And I'm guessing that could go right up to that old crush you had back in junior high if you never actually had a relationship after that.

Of course, if you just happen to be the person that's never fallen for anyone… It would likely be a pretty much exact duplicate of #1, an ideal which you have never actually seen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like, and think it stands for itself currently. My suggestion for improvement is to follow up with an interview about what the other two missing are like, from the months old vague memories of the gallery patrons. It shouldn't be explicit, maybe even just (EXPUNGE) the whole thing, but it is something the Foundation would certainly do in a routine investigation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oooh, you added two more mystery paintings. Intriguing. I wonder what they do?
My guess: One shows your favorite food personified, while the other shows you what you'd look like as a muppet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to use #4 as a passive agressive note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addendum added, based on further consideration and an outside suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm guessing someone with a narcissic personality disorder would probably just see themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone with visual agnosia?

Also, what if someone doesn't have a "Significant Other"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw it post-edit, and I'm gonna + it up. And I kind of like not having TOO much description involved with how different personality types see themselves; I have a lovely little mental image of a severe narcissist seeing #4 and utterly losing his shit, someone with extreme self-image issues seeing #2 and becoming MORE despondent, etc.

Of course here's one; what kind of person would it take to look at the "self-image" ones…. And see nothing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have to concur. I was moderately interested, and then the article was over and I was going, "…and?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm kinda curious as to how people with certain psychological problem would view the "see yourself" painting. Specifically, would people with severe narcissistic personality disorder or severe depression see themselves any better/worse/differently than they already do? Note that I'm not asking for you to explain whether or not this is the case; it's just something that I'm curious about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited per advice provided by Lasergoose and Sorts. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me, what works the most about the concept is that the painting is not showing you something you didn't already know or think.

Everything you'd see in any painting is taken straight from your own thoughts, and the whole idea of a person not being prepared to admit or face their inner thoughts is a time honored concept in horror and drama.

I almost think I'd like it better if people were disappointed by what they saw, either because the real world will never match a person's desires or because they realize they aren't getting anything new… or something like "I was expecting to see my ultimate desire and all I got is Bea Arthur in a bathtub filled with chocolate pudding"

I mean that's fantastic and all but at some point you are going to get sick of chocolate pudding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By all means. I personally hope this survives. It's at -3 right now, and I'm about to upvote because I like it … but that little horizontal dash can be hard to overcome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the input, Lasergoose. I'll edit. Can I use your words directly? That is a good modification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the note makes it more obvious that these are potentially dangerous, but that should probably be mentioned in the description itself.
I don't think the note is unnecessarily humorous, but it is a lot wordier than necessary. If you really want to write a note to show the effect the paintings have had on staff, I'd suggest probably just date it and phrase it as professionally as possible - like, "Following investigation into 4 staff suicides and multiple emotional problems reported to staff psychologists, non D-class personnel are barred from directly viewing any of the SCP-1236 set of paintings."
Again, I'd try writing in the dangers right into the description, and then evaluating whether you even need the note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. It's not bad enough for me to downvote, but not good enough for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I concur. Humorous addenda really annoy me when they boil down to 'stop misusing this!' or 'don't prank people with this!'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Well, it's not bad. There's something about the idea of paintings that are such blank slates that the viewer impresses their own desires or fears upon but the execution leaves something to be desired.

At the very least, get rid of the ol' "stop playing with the SCP" note at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I have been writing more intensive reviews lately, this one gets a resounding 'Meh.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New SCP posted on 12/12/11.
